era_context: Late Bronze Age collapse period (c. 1200–1050 BCE). The Eastern Mediterranean and Near East are in the midst of systemic disruption. Egypt under Ramesses III remains a major power but faces mounting pressures. The Hittite Empire has recently collapsed; Mycenaean civilization is in terminal decline; the Iron Age is beginning in some regions. Cuneiform literacy is fragmenting; palace-centered economies are collapsing.
geographic_coverage_gaps: East Asia (Chinese records exist but are not well-integrated into Western historiography for this specific year)
Mesoamerica (no written records; archaeological dating uncertain)

events.title: Ramesses III's Tenth Regnal Year; Reign Continues Amid Crisis
events.description: Ramesses III continues his reign as pharaoh of Egypt's 20th Dynasty. This year falls within the documented period of his rule (c. 1186–1155 BCE). His reign is characterized by military campaigns against the Sea Peoples, internal economic strain, labor unrest (including the famous strikes of Deir el-Medina workers), and administrative fragmentation. No specific event is recorded for this exact year, but the broader context of his reign is well-documented.

sources.name: Medinet Habu temple inscriptions and reliefs
Papyrus Harris I and Deir el-Medina papyri

events.certainty_note: Ramesses III's regnal years are well-established through temple inscriptions and papyri. This year is assigned based on regnal chronology (tenth year of his reign), but no specific event is dated to 1142 BCE.

events.title: Late Bronze Age Collapse: Continued Disruption of Mediterranean Trade and Settlement Patterns
events.description: The collapse of Late Bronze Age palatial economies and trade networks continues. Major centers in Cyprus, the Levant, and Anatolia show evidence of abandonment or severe disruption. Long-distance trade connections that had linked Egypt, the Hittite Empire, Mycenaean Greece, and the Levantine city-states are fragmenting. Localized, subsistence-based settlements replace centralized palace economies. This year falls within the broader period of systemic collapse.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Cyprus, the Levant, Anatolia, and Greece (settlement patterns, pottery typology, absence of palatial administrative records)
Historiographic consensus on Late Bronze Age collapse

events.certainty_note: The Late Bronze Age collapse is dated c. 1200–1050 BCE. This year falls within that window, but no specific event is datable to 1142 BCE.

disconfirming_evidence: Modern scholarship has substantially revised the traditional narrative of the Late Bronze Age collapse. Rather than a sudden, externally-driven catastrophe caused by 'Sea Peoples' invasions, recent work emphasizes multiple, interconnected causes: climate change (drought), systems collapse, internal instability, and migration. The 'Sea Peoples' are now understood as one symptom of broader disruption rather than the primary cause. The collapse was not simultaneous across all regions; some areas (Egypt, Assyria) survived with reduced power. No specific event is recorded for 1142 BCE, making it impossible to identify disconfirming evidence for this particular year.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1142 BCE is extremely sparse. Knowledge of this period comes almost entirely from Egyptian temple inscriptions, papyri, and archaeological evidence. The Egyptian sources are biased toward royal and administrative perspectives and tend to emphasize military victories while downplaying internal economic crisis. The archaeological record is incomplete and subject to dating uncertainty (±20–30 years for this period). Non-Egyptian civilizations left few written records for this year; knowledge of them is almost entirely archaeological. Specific events for individual years are rarely datable.

graph_edges.note: Ramesses III's reign spans multiple years; these entries document consecutive years of his rule.
The Late Bronze Age collapse is a continuous process; these entries document consecutive years within that collapse.
